dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a 69-year-old reporting abdominal pain.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. Can you tell me what brought you to call 911?
I have pain in my stomach, and I just threw up. This started about an hour ago.
You mentioned stomach pain, vomiting, and shortness of breath. Are you also experiencing nausea and diarrhea?
Your airway appears patent and self-maintained.
I can see that your breathing is very fast and labored. Do you feel like you're having trouble breathing?
I'll now listen to your lungs. Is that okay with you?
I heard some rhonchi initially, but after CPAP, the airflow has improved.
I'll now take your blood pressure. Hold this arm still for a second.
In your medical history, it notes that you have primary hypertension. Do you take medicine for it?
I usually take them daily, but I'm not sure if I took them today.
When did the symptoms start today?
On a scale of 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst pain you've ever had, how would you rate your pain right now?
Does the pain spread anywhere else in your body?
Your pulse is 69 beats per minute.
Your breathing is 36 per minute and labored.
Blood pressure is recorded at 208 over 98.
Your oxygen level is 82 percent on room air.
Your blood glucose level was not obtained due to initial equipment limitations.
I'm going to begin assisted ventilation with CPAP to help improve your breathing.
CPAP is set to 7.5 cmH2O with 30% FiO2 and PEEP applied. Does this feel okay?
Your eyes appear PERRL and you are alert and oriented to four areas.
Let me palpate your chest and check your breathing again.
Now placing 4-lead EKG electrodes to monitor your heart rate.
The machine shows your heart in a normal sinus rhythm.
We will now take a 12-lead EKG. You should not feel any pain from this.
No signs of jugular venous distension were noted during the neck exam.
Paramedic [NAME] will finalize our interventions and contact the hospital.
Let me take your blood pressure and heart rate again.
Your heart rate has increased to 86 beats per minute.
Blood pressure is 168 over 66 now.
Your oxygen level has increased to 94 percent.
Your CPAP settings are still in place. How's that working for you?
On a scale of 0 to 10, how would you rate your pain right now?
The pain has gone, or maybe it's around zero.
Are there any new or worsening symptoms right now?
You are being transported to [COMPANY_NAME] for further evaluation.
You'll be placed on the stretcher in a semi-fowler's position with five straps for safety.
We've followed Protocol 3-11 for suspected respiratory distress.
I will provide a complete report when we arrive at the hospital.
I just observed your respirations dropping to 2, is this correct?
Is your CPAP still helping? Any issues with breathing?
No, it's still good, and I can breathe better now.
Do you currently experience any discomfort?
No, everything feels okay now.
At this time, you remain alert and oriented with no signs of immediate danger.
Paramedics have followed the Respiratory Distress protocol (Protocol 3-11) for suspected pulmonary edema.
We have arrived at [COMPANY_NAME], where RN [NAME] will receive your report.
The receiving nurse has confirmed all critical findings and will continue your care.
You have no known allergies. Have you ever had an allergic reaction to medications or foods?
You mentioned not taking your daily medication today. Can you provide the name of your medication?
No rectal bleeding or urinary issues found on examination.
All of your extremities appear intact and you have good movement.
Your pulse is strong and regular with no signs of poor circulation.
Skin appears warm and dry with easily palpable peripheral pulses.
Current concern for pulmonary edema remains, with acute respiratory distress.
No distension or masses found during abdominal palpation. It remains soft and non-tender.
No signs of neck stiffness or masses found during examination.
Your mental status remains alert and responsive with orientation to time, place, and person, and to your location.
Are you still in sinus rhythm with the 12-lead EKG in place?
Yes, we continue to show mild ST depression and a stable sinus rhythm.
CPAP settings remain at 7.5 cmH2O and 30% FiO2, with no changes needed.
I'll monitor your vitals again in one minute.
I've just confirmed a new 4-lead tracing for continued monitoring.
Can you inform me when you last had a drink or meal?
Could you tell me exactly what happened before you started having these symptoms?
Current concern for pulmonary edema remains, with acute respiratory distress.
No rashes, petechiae, or unusual marks found during the physical examination.
No nasal obstruction or visible foreign body noted.
Let me listen to your breath sounds again under the CPAP.
Your breathing appears better than before and more consistent.
I've just confirmed a new 4-lead tracing for continued monitoring.
Are you on any other medications besides what we discussed?
No, I take only the daily medication for my hypertension, and I didn't take it today.
Patient has a known history of hypertension but skipped a dose of prescribed medication.
Unable to collect personal information due to patient distress.
You are currently secured using five straps in semi-fowler's position.
Hospital ED was contacted by Paramedic [NAME] in advance of arrival without orders.
Patient remains on the Respiratory Distress protocol, with no cardiac compromise noted.
Continuous-flow oxygen via CPAP remains the only intervention.
I will reassess your oxygen saturation again to confirm optimal CPAP use.
Are you comfortable moving now? Partner: Patient gave a thumbs up.
Five-person team carried you to the stretcher and placed rails up in the semi-fowler position.
EKG settings will be adjusted for final monitoring before arrival at the hospital.
Earlier, you said you had no chest pain. Is that still accurate?
Yes, no chest pain - only my stomach and breathing are affected today.
Let me re-check your heart rate one more time before arrival.
Heart rate is now 86 beats per minute and stable.
Your Glasgow Coma Score remains at 15, with alertness and PERRL pupils.
No evidence of cyanosis or absent distal pulses noted.
Patient is now placed in ED bed 21 for further evaluation.
Based on your 12-lead EKG and chest exam, there is no immediate cardiac compromise.
I have ensured all documentation was handed over to hospital staff.
Receiving RN signed documentation after review.
Full handoff now given to ED, and agreement to continue CPAP as per your current protocol.
This marks the final handoff as the hospital assumes care for the patient.
